abstract:
Maintaining high water quality is essential for human health, biodiversity, industrial processes, and environmental stability. However, recent decades have seen a significant decline in water safety due to rising contamination and pollution. This study addresses these challenges by developing robust predictive frameworks for the Water Quality Index (WQI) and Water Quality Classification (WQC), which serve as critical metrics for determining water validity. To enhance predictive performance, we employed grid search optimization to fine-tune the hyperparameters of eight different machine learning models. For WQC prediction, we evaluated four classification algorithms: Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost). For WQI estimation, we implemented four regression models: K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Decision Tree (DT), Support Vector Regressor (SVR), and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP).

The study utilized a dataset comprising 1,991 instances and 7 features, which underwent rigorous preprocessing, including mean imputation for missing values and data normalization to ensure consistency and model suitability. The classification models were evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC), and F1-score, while regression performance was measured through Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Median Absolute Error (MedAE), Mean Square Error (MSE), and the coefficient of determination (R²). Experimental results demonstrate that the Gradient Boosting model achieved the highest classification accuracy at 99.50%. In terms of regression, the MLP regressor outperformed its counterparts, achieving an exceptional R² value of 99.8%. These findings highlight the efficacy of optimized machine learning architectures in monitoring and managing water quality resources.
